coredata.dc:description: Owing to massive global energy claims and up-to-date environmental issues, the necessity to realize renewable, abundant, efficient, and low-cost energy sources is urgently required. Solar energy is a favorable source that is expected to fulfill the future energy demand all over the world. Organic/inorganic hybrid perovskites are good solar harvesting materials that recently gained intensive attention due to their high absorption coefficient, broadly tunable bandgap, incredible charge mobility, long charge diffusion lengths, and long carrier lifetime. Interestingly, over the past ten years, perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have demonstrated striking competitive power conversion efficiency (PCE) of over 25%. Despite its rapid progress, a few crucial challenges prevent this ground-breaking technology—which can be summed up as long-lasting stability—from being commercialized. A range of carbon-based materials (CBMs) such as fullerenes, graphene, graphene derivatives, carbon dots, graphene quantum dots, and carbon nanotubes are used extensively in PSCs to improve their performance, especially stability, because they are inexpensive, easily processed, stable, and have unique optoelectronic properties. In this regard, the most recent developments regarding the use of CBMs as interface layers, electron transport layers (ETLs), hole transport layers (HTLs), additives to perovskite layers, as well as counter electrodes are examined in detail. Furthermore, the improved stability as a result of using CBMs in PSCs is emphasized. Last but not least, we address future perspectives on carbon-based PSCs, focusing on existing challenges and potential solutions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to massive global energy claims and up-to-date environmental issues, the necessity to realize renewable, abundant, efficient, and low-cost energy sources is urgently required. Solar energy is a favorable source that is expected to fulfill the future energy demand all over the world. Organic/inorganic hybrid perovskites are good solar harvesting materials that recently gained intensive attention due to their high absorption coefficient, broadly tunable bandgap, incredible charge mobility, long charge diffusion lengths, and long carrier lifetime. Interestingly, over the past ten years, perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have demonstrated striking competitive power conversion efficiency (PCE) of over 25%. Despite its rapid progress, a few crucial challenges prevent this ground-breaking technology—which can be summed up as long-lasting stability—from being commercialized. A range of carbon-based materials (CBMs) such as fullerenes, graphene, graphene derivatives, carbon dots, graphene quantum dots, and carbon nanotubes are used extensively in PSCs to improve their performance, especially stability, because they are inexpensive, easily processed, stable, and have unique optoelectronic properties. In this regard, the most recent developments regarding the use of CBMs as interface layers, electron transport layers (ETLs), hole transport layers (HTLs), additives to perovskite layers, as well as counter electrodes are examined in detail. Furthermore, the improved stability as a result of using CBMs in PSCs is emphasized. Last but not least, we address future perspectives on carbon-based PSCs, focusing on existing challenges and potential solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: The main component and means of reducing climate change is energy. Large amounts of greenhouse gases are produced during the energy production process, such as when fossil fuels are burned to produce heat and electricity. Countries all over the world have to get rid of the worst impacts associated with climate change. We have to lower the half percentage of green gas emissions by 2030 and reach zero emissions by 2050 [1]. To achieve this, we have to reduce the reliance on fossil fuels and invest in sustainable, affordable, accessible, and clean alternate sources of energy like solar energy, wind energy, and hydroelectric power. Therefore, in order to meet future energy demands, new options are offered for the development of renewable energy utilization technology, such as solar cells. Over the years, three generations of solar cells have been introduced and great efforts have been made to achieve highly efficient photovoltaics [2]. Numerous criteria must be satisfied regarding photovoltaic (PV) technology commercialization, including low-cost, high-power conversion efficiency (PCE), long-term stability, and low toxicity.

The perovskite solar cells (PSCs), a category of the third generation solar cells, have received incredible attention due to their outstanding PV performance and their fascinating PCE that significantly increased from 3.1 % to over 25 % for single junction and reached 31.3 % for the tandem multi-junction configuration, demonstrating their massive potential [3–5]. The compelling advantages of PSCs are driving their revolution: a tunable band-gap range (1.2 to 2.3 eV); an exceptional light absorption coefficient due to s-p anti-bonding coupling, which results in a thinner perovskite layer within the PSC device that is approximately 400 nm in size; a long charge-carrier lifetime exceeding 100 ns; greater structural defect tolerance; a lower rate of recombination at the surface; a diffusion length exceeding 1000 nm; and ambipolar characteristics [6–8]. Basically, PSCs consist of an organic–inorganic halide perovskite with ABX3 configuration where (A = methylammonium (MA) and/or formamidinium (FA), Cs+ and/or Rb+; B = Pb2+, Sn2+, and X = I−, Br−) as the light absorber layer. Generally, the perovskite layer is sandwiched between the p-type hole-transport layer (HTL) and the n-type electron-transport layer (ETL) [9]. To get optimum charge extraction, matching of valence and conduction bands of the perovskite with p- and n-type charge selective layers is very essential: it’s a must that the ETL conduction band (CB) edge be lower than that of the perovskite and the HTL valence band (VB) edge to be higher than the VB of the perovskite [10]. Therefore, optimizing the charge transport layers in terms of optoelectronic properties improves the charge carrier separation. HTL facilitates transporting the hole from the perovskite absorber layer into the back electrode while also serving as an electron-blocking layer to inhibit electron flow. Typically, organic hole transporting materials commonly employed are spiro-OMeTAD (2,20,7,70-tetrakis-(N,N-di-p-methoxyphenylamine)-9,90-spirobiuorene) and P3HT (Poly(3-hexylthiophene −2,5-diyl)), while inorganic materials are like CuI (Copper(I) iodide), NiO (Nickel oxide) [11,12] and CuSCN [13], AgSCN [14]. For ETL, this layer assists the injected electrons from the perovskite absorber layer into the front electrode and acting as a hole-blocking layer to suppress the release of the holes to the front electrode. The typical electron transport materials are TiO2, ZnO, SnO2, IGZO, and PCBM [15]. In addition, there are different PSCs configurations: planar configuration, based on the various positions of ETL and HTL, normal (n-i-p) and planar inverted (p-i-n); mesoporous PSCs and HTM-free [16].

From the mass production point of view, there are three determining elements for the scalability of a solar cell: device competence, cost, and stability. To date, achieving long-term stability and ensuring consistent outdoor operation remains the primary obstacle to the mass production of PSCs. The high sensitivity of the perovskite layer and associated contact layers to environmental factors like moisture, oxygen, temperature, and UV light is the reason for the instability in PSCs [17]. Indeed, the fabrication techniques and type of perovskite layer play a significant role in the device’s stability. Tailoring new materials with superior structural and optoelectronic properties to match the specific requirements for industrial applications gained so far is an intensive effort from researchers worldwide. Carbon-based materials (CBMs) have been receiving a lot of attention lately in the fields of industrial development and materials science [18–20]. These materials include a new variety of CBMs, such as graphene and its derivatives, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), fullerenes, carbon nanofiber (CNF), and carbon dots, as shown in Fig. 1
[21]. These CBMs possess extraordinary properties such as chemical stability, flexibility, thermal, superior optical, electrical conductivity, high surface area, mechanical properties, and low cost [22]. A significant advancement in solar cell technology is expected when multiple CBMs are incorporated into the PSCs. This will eventually result in lower manufacturing costs, more stability, and a range of device designs that make the CBMs appealing for the process of commercialization. We will assess the most recent developments in the use of CBMs as ETLs, HTLs, interface layers, additives, and counter electrodes in PSCs. Furthermore, improving the device’s performance from the standpoint of CBMs, specifically with regard to stability will be scrutinized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Breakthrough of C-based materials in PSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Despite the remarkable intensive development in the PSCs, that has been recently achieved within the last decade on the laboratory scale, there are definite concerns that still must be taken into concern such as the low-cost, long-term constancy, environmentally friendly materials, and the wide-scale commercialization [19,23,24]. Based on the preceding introduction, carbon-based nanomaterials scoping from the zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots to the two-dimensional 2D graphene derivatives passing through one-dimensional carbon nanotubes have been recently utilized in the diverse components of the perovskite-based devices [25]. Those materials can boost the efficiency and stability of PSCs as they exhibit remarkable electrical, and mechanical properties, and unique chemical and physical stability [26–28]. Owing to the outstanding charge transportation mobility, tunable Fermi level configuration and low-cost preparation can be utilized as additives or alternative function layers throughout the PSCs architecture in addition to being counter electrodes. As well as exhibit water-resistant characteristics, they can suppress the degradation of different function layers and make it an effective protective layer for the PSCs films in moisture environmental conditions [29], consequently enhancing the long-term stability of the PSCs. Upon incorporation of graphene into Poly(3-hexylthiophene-2,5-diyl) (P3HT) has significantly improved the charge mobility and obtained a better hole transport than pure (P3HT) [30,31]. A certified PCE of 17 % was recorded. Moreover, the device stability was enhanced by only 3 % drop after storage in (humidity 50 %) for 1680 h without encapsulation. While storage in a nitrogen environment for 600 h under 1-sun illumination at room temperature, this device retained 89 % of its original output with encapsulation [32]. However, the counter electrodes composed of CBMs exhibit lower conductivity compared to the common Au, Ag and Al metal-based electrodes. Thus, numerous initiatives are still being made to produce electrodes with reduced resistivity. While the hydrophobic property is necessary to prevent perovskite films from degrading by shielding them from external moisture and enhancing their long-term stability. The hydrophilic property is also necessary to ensure that the carbon nanomaterials are well-interacted with the various other layers in the perovskite matrix to produce flexible devices [33]. Consequently, for continued development, it is necessary to address the balance between hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity for the carbon nanomaterials in the PSCs.

In the following sections, we will discuss in more detail the extensive research efforts that have been made to improve and develop carbon-based nanomaterial-assisted PSCs. We find that scientists have used carbon nanomaterials of different dimensions in the structure of PSCs to promote further progress in terms of efficiency and stability. So specifically, the advantages and limitations of applying these materials as functional or auxiliary layers as well as counter electrodes along with their long-term stability will be discussed extensively.

CNTs are carbon allotropes that can be imagined as rolled graphene sheets in a cylindrical shape, either as single-wall CNTs (SWCNT) or multi-wall CNTs (MWCNT). Depending on the rolling angle, the CNTs can be identified by so-called chiral indices, through which the CNTs can be classified as metallic or semiconductors. CNTs exhibiting a semiconducting character could be used as charge-transporting layers due to the formation of Schottky barriers between CNTs and the semiconductor. CNTs exhibit high carrier mobility, about 80,000–100,000 cm2 V−1 s−1 [34], chemical and mechanical stability, and are naturally hydrophobic, enabling CNTs to be a promising alternative for unstable organic HTL [35]. Currently, CNTs are employed as additives for charge-transporting layers embedded into the perovskite film or the back electrode [20]. Table 1 displays the impact of CNTs on the performance and durability of PSCs.

Tailoring the charge extraction layers is critical to obtaining a better current collection. Typically, the electron extraction is assured by employing of semiconducting oxides like SnO2, TiO2, and ZnO [36]. The ETL should be pinhole-free, high quality, and form a well-coated substrate to prevent the direct contact of perovskite with the electrodes and prevent charge recombination. In order to enhance the electron collection efficiency, one of the interesting approaches is the insertion of CNT into the structure of the semiconductor. ZnO is a wide-bandgap semiconductor with significant electron mobility and high light absorption [37]. Mohammed et al. have doped ZnO with different ratios of CNT to inhibit the hydroxyl groups adsorbed on the oxide surface responsible for the perovskite deterioration, Fig. 2
[38]. An efficiency increase was observed from 9 % to 15 % after adding the CNT. For stability, the fabricated device maintained about 82 % after aging for 2000 h (two months) in ambient air at 30 % humidity without encapsulation. Uthayaraj Siva et al. studied the effect of adding CNTs on both ETL and HTL, TiO2 and Poly (3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT), respectively [39]. The obtained device gave better FF and 14 % PCE and eliminated charge recombination. The effect of Multi-wall Carbon nanotubes MWCNT on the optoelectronic properties of the m-TiO2 ETL-based PSC was investigated [40]. Batmunkh et al. used 0.1 wt% SWCNTs in TiO2 porous films to enhance electron transport and reduce carrier recombination, resulting in an increase in JSC from 19.0 to 21.6 mA cm−2. SWCNTs also shifted the conduction band minimum of TiO2 to form a suitable band energy alignment, increasing VOC to 1.002 V from 0.986 V, resulting in a PCE of 16.11 %. Also, in Mohammed et al. study the power conversion energy was boosted from 12.5 % to 21.4 % upon insertion of MWCNT into m-TiO2 film due to providing bigger grains with better light absorption. The device retained about 82 % of the initial performance after aging in moist conditions and about 80 % at room temperature [41]. Uthayaraj Siva et al. studied the effect of adding CNTs on both ETL and HTL, TiO2 and Poly (3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT), respectively [39]. The obtained device gave better FF and 14 % PCE, eliminating charge recombination. Guang Shao et al. used functionalized carbon nanotubes (CNTs) to chemically connect the SnO2/perovskite interface, which influenced both trap states and energy levels [42]. According to theory and experiments, CNTs operate as electron donors to SnO2, and the Pb-cluster-based perovskite, which results in n-type doping of SnO2 and transfers the Pb-cluster's 6p-orbitals to the conduction band (CB) edge, changes the deep trap states to shallower ones. The interface linker increases the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of PSC devices by 24.04 % while maintaining a high fill factor of 83 %. Furthermore, the PSCs have higher operational and thermal stability, and we show that after 1000 h of sun illumination at 65 °C heating, the device retains 88.15 % of its initial efficiency. Virendra Kumar et al. modified titanium dioxide (TiO2) films by incorporating multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). MWCNT-modified TiO2 films exhibit better optical properties and surface growth than pristine TiO2 films. Enhanced optical absorbance in MAPbI3 films had been observed on perovskite films fabricated onto MWCNT-modified TiO2 films with enlarged grains and a red shift in optical band gap has been observed for MWCNT-modified TiO2 films and MWCNT-modified TiO2/MAPbI3 films. Further, various critical electrical parameters such as charge carrier concentration, Hole mobility, sheet resistance, and resistivity have been investigated to understand the effects of the incorporation of MWCNT on TiO2 films. The value of carrier concentration enhances from 3.76 × 1024 cm−3 to 6.39 × 1024 cm−3 on incorporation of MWCNT in TiO2 films. Furthermore, an enhancement in crystallinity, optical absorbance, and grain size has been observed in perovskite films fabricated onto TiO2/MWCNT films [43]. Mansureh Roohollahi et al. presented a simulation and optimization approach for PSCs, incorporating a carbon nanotube (CNT) layer as a charge collector on the cathode side. The high conductivity of the CNT layer facilitates rapid electron transfer to the cathode. The CNT layer creates a low-resistance pathway for charge collection on the cathode side, preventing charge accumulation and reducing recombination, thereby improving the external quantum efficiency and achieving a remarkable increase in the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the conventional PSC structure, reaching 22.80 % [44].

Huijie Tang et al. fabricated a hybrid ETL of SnO2 and CNTs by thermally decomposing a mixture of SnCl4·5H2O and pretreated CNTs (SnO2-CNT). The inclusion of CNTs can greatly increase the conductivity of SnO2 films while decreasing the trap-state density, hence facilitating carrier passage from the perovskite layer to the cathode [45]. The hysteresis-free PSCs based on SnO2-CNT ETL reach a high efficiency of 20.33 %, which is 13.58 % higher than the typical device (power conversion efficiency = 17.90 %). Syed Muhammad Zain Mehdi et al. developed efficient perovskite solar cells with PCBM and Cr2O3@GP and Cr2O3@CNT as the ETL. Hybrid ETLs with Cr2O3@GP and Cr2O3@CNT exhibited improved PCE of 18.5 % and 26.8 % as compared to plane ETL [46]. When Cr2O3@GP/CNT was added to the ETL, the ETL conductivity significantly improved, and the series resistance declined. In addition, after a 336-hour stability measurement, the hybrid ETL still had almost 96 % of its initial PCE. Janendra Pratap et al. proposed a device structure of PSC (ITO/SWCNT/CH3NH3SnI3/Cz-P/Pd). In this device, SWCNT is utilized as ETL. The optimized results for the proposed device with Voc of 0.998 V, Jsc of 38.77 mA/cm2, FF of 77.46 % and PCE of 29.99 % were achieved [47].

Modifying the structure and composition of the ETL, HTL and employing novel strategies have lately received noteworthy interest in improving the photovoltaic performance within the PSCs devices. The hole-transporting materials, especially the expensive Spiro-OMeTAD and its doped forms, have an easy performance degradation mechanism hampering the PSCs from commercialization. Owing to the charge mobility lack and the presence of micro- and macro pinholes within Spiro-OMeTAD that induce gas diffusion [48], it could not be used individually; additives are required to be further doped like4-test-butyl pyridine (tBP) and lithium bis(trifluoromethane sulfonyl)imide (LiTFSi). Nevertheless, these additives suppress the device stability through corrosion of the perovskite film by the TBP [49,50], for example. The hygroscopic nature of the Li-TFSI results in itself-dissolving in the presence of moisture; besides, any residuals of the acetonitrile solvent used in dissolving Li-TFSI that causes damage to the perovskite layer [51].

In contrast, inorganic p-type materials have also been utilized as HTL in various forms like nanotubes, quantum dots or nanocrystals due to their high efficiency and economic cost. Continuous development in efficiency and long-term stability are the recent crucial issues. However, some points should be considered [52]; the perovskite/HTM contact should be controlled to address the interface charge recombination. In addition, the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) energy levels of the p-type-based HTL and the perovskite valence band (VB) should be matched to improve the long diffusion pathway. Great efforts have been made to utilize CNTs in HTL of PSC through various configurations. However, employing CNTs as a sole HTL provides neither sufficient charge selection nor generation of enough consolidated −fields that avoid interfacial recombination losses [53]. This problem can be addressed by p-doping to overcome these obstacles. On the other hand, applying the CNTs as an interlayer facilitates the funneling charge extraction into the HTL and thus eliminates interface-charge recombination.

To adjust the energy band alignment and enhance the interface between the perovskite layer and carbon-based materials, Nan Jiang, et al. employed CNTs into a dopant-free Spiro-OMeTAD hole transporting layer [54]. Insertion of CNTs enhanced the conductivity of Spiro-OMeTAD resulting in outstanding charge transfer compared to the non-doped Spiro-OMeTAD leading to a PCE value of 10.45 %. For inverted PSCs, the metal oxides-based HTL usually exhibits low conductivity. Hence a hybrid composed of CNTs and NiOx with different concentrations of CNT ranging from (0–20 vol%) was employed [55]. By introducing CNTs into NiO- matrix, the NiOx conductivity increased while the charge recombination decreased, which was confirmed by electrochemical impedance [56]. The PCE increased from 13.1 −15.1 % upon insertion of CNTs into the HTL. The best-optimized cell was 7.5 vol%-CNTs, which gives a PCE of up to 16.9 %. Z. Shadrokh and their co-workers have incorporated CNTs into quaternary chalcogenide Cu2ZnSnS4 (CZTS) structure as a HTL as well as the polymeric poly(4-vinylpyridine) (PVP) interlayer on the top of the perovskite film [34] to address the instability and the high-cost organic HTL materials, Fig. 3
(a-c). The PV parameters have shown increments in a PCE value of 15.2 % which can be attributed to the presence of highly conductive CNT that increase the Jsc and the FF values and the turnout of PVP interlayer that mainly passivates the surface trap states and, thus obtaining higher Voc, as well. These hydrophobic CNTs boosted the device's stability for 30 days in 45 % humidity at room temperature, maintaining about 98 % of its initial PCE. Despite the enhanced stability recently observed for the C-based PSC, it still suffers from low power conversion due to the insufficient charge extraction at the perovskite/carbon interface. Xiaoli Zheng and his co-workers have taken a different way by inserting boron B into the MWCNTs electrode resulting in better hole extraction. They're transporting through improving the work function and the conductivity of the CNTs. The fabricated device improved considerably in PCE to reach 14.6 % with negligible hysteresis. For mentioning the long-term stability, the results recorded maintaining about 98 % of the initial performance after 80 days of aging in dry air. While applying thermal stressing at 80°C and 65 % humidity, the performance decreased by 15 % and 7 %, respectively, after two weeks [57]. Yu Lu and their co-workers [58] have fabricated a novel binaphthylamine-based HTL with different side chains. The two optimized HTL materials included amine and methyl units, physiochemically adsorbed on the CNT for inverted perovskite configuration Fig. 3 (d). The Z7-based HTL (depends on the dihedral angle between the two naphthyl units) resulted in 17.3 %, comparable to that with MWCNT (17.1 %). However, it exhibited much higher stability than the pristine one within storage in ambient air without encapsulation for 14 days. Better energy level alignment and, thus, facile extraction of holes have been obtained upon using Cr2O3-MWCNT as HTL, which boosted the PCE to 16.29 % [59]. Insertion of Cr2O3 into MWCNT enhanced the hydrophobicity and overwhelmed the hysteresis and the charge recombination at the borders, leading to a well long-term stability. Only a 0.32 % drop of PCE was observed within 50 days under illumination and moisture of 40 %.

Like Au and Ag, the commonly used metal electrodes are deposited through high vacuum thermal evaporation conditions, which are not cost-effective for wide-scale applications at all, despite their low resistivity and high reflectivity. Moreover, Ag-CEs could deteriorate the device stability by forming Ag-halides with the perovskite layer [60]. Generally, back electrodes needed suitable work function for better alignment between the energy levels and the VB of the perovskite or the HOMO of the HTL. To get perfect contact between the CE and the perovskite, the electrode conductivity and morphology have to be seriously considered, which greatly affects the overall PSC performance [61]. Carbon-based electrodes have been intensively studied as counter electrodes CEs for dye-sensitized solar cells, which provide comparable PCE to Pt-based ones. For the PSCs, carbon-based electrodes achieved better stability than metal CEs. These can be acknowledged for their inert nature to the environment and the perovskite film, which provides a larger area covering the perovskite than metal CEs. CNTs exhibit good thermal stability and excellent electrical conductivity in addition to their hydrophobic nature. Furthermore, CNTs possess suitable work functions ranging from 4.7-5.1 eV [62]; these characteristics enable CNTs to be employed as CEs. In this section, we introduce the recent advance in CNTs as a back electrode. However, the insulated CNTs have usually been applied as CEs for the conventional PSCs, and p-type CNTs give more efficiency and better mobility, enabling them to be efficient additives for the perovskite.

Hybrid graphene with CNT film was employed to replace the expensive noble metal counter electrode by Mengdi Tian, et al [63]. The obtained device showed enhanced stability, maintaining about 86 % of the premier performance (15 %) after 500 h storage in the atmosphere with a relatively high humidity of 50 %. They studied the effect of the number of graphene layers on the PSCs performance. The hybrid graphene-4/CNT-8 film revealed the best performance among all the investigated PSCs. High conductive film results exhibit better efficiency and stability due to multi-graphene layers and the CNT that act as a block for external moisture. Fig. 4
(a-c) shows the schematic fabrication of the hybrid film, the water contact angle and the PCE stability curve. Shuang Liu replaced the carbon-black/graphite-electrode with a composite of NiO and SWCNT to fabricate an HTL-free perovskite solar cell [64,65]. By conjoining mesoscopic TiO2/Al2O3-based ETL with NiO/SWCNT as a CE, the PCE reached 12.7 % with better photocurrent delivery. Moreover, by utilizing the SWCNT, the CE's thickness can be reduced by maintaining the counter electrode conductivity. For the presence of NiO and SWCNT, n-type semiconductors, their valence bands match with the MAPbI3 that facilitates the hole extraction at the perovskite/CE interface. Here, the shelf-time for investigating the device stability showed a PCE of 12.4 % after 300 h storage in 40–60 % humidity at RT without illumination. In 2018, Yue Wang et al. utilized SWCNT as a bridging approach into the perovskite and the carbon layers to enhance the contact at the perovskite /C interface and thus not only boost the conductivity of CE but also facilitate the hole withdrawal at the interface [66]. In this HTL-free fabricated device, Fig. 4 (d-g), CNTs can penetrate the MAPbI3 and the carbon cathode; therefore, the charge extraction and transporting are improved. SWCNTs are embedded into the counter electrode via past containing three different types of graphite (flaky graphite (12500 Mesh), spheroidal graphite (8 μm), and flaky graphite (15000 mesh)). The good outstanding long-term stability is observed after aging for 90 days without encapsulation in 65 % humidity in ambient air and then at a temperature of 75 °C, resulting in maintaining almost 100 % of the original performance (15 %). Moreover, when carbon nanotubes CNTs/carbon fiber CF were employed as CE for HTL-free PSC, the interface area between perovskite and carbon surface increased [67]. The obtained device enhanced the hole extraction and the hole transfer acceleration due to the CNT structure's high pathway; therefore, the photoluminescence (PL) peak intensity decreased. The yield PCE was 11.8 %, reflecting on the device stability to maintain about 83 % of the initial PCE in the dark ambient conditions of 40 % humidity for 30 days.

However, the entirely inorganic-based PSCs exhibit good chemical stability; the efficiency values are uncompetitive due to the poor contact at the interface of the perovskite/C-based electrodes. To overcome this problem, Yinglong Yang and their co-workers fabricated polyethyleneimine PEI/functionalized-CNT interlayer for CsPbI3-based C-PSCs [68]. This PEI/CNT bridge works on improving the perovskite/carbon contact, besides acting as a passivation interface for the perovskite layer due to the coordination characteristics of the PEI (lone pairs of –NH2) with Pb ions of the perovskite as displayed in Fig. 5 . A remarkable increase in the PCE from 7.41 % to 10.55 % was introduced, while the FF was improved from 0.56 to 0.71. Moreover, the surface trap states decreased, in addition to increasing the charge transfer. The non-encapsulated device showed enhanced long-term stability that can sustain about 80 % from the initial PCE under high humidity of 70 % and high temperature of 80 °C in air atmosphere for two weeks. In 2022, CNT was employed as top electrodes with the incorporation of MoO3 doping layers, enhancing their performance [69]. This strategy worked on improving energy alignment, p-doping and hole collection efficiency. The authors found that an excess of MoO3 resulted in misalignment in the energy levels and damaged the perovskite layer, while a lower amount diminished the p-doping and hole extraction. The highest obtained PCE (17.3 %) was achieved by utilizing a CNT-T70% PS, where T stands for transmittance.

The fabrication of foldable devices has great attention recently in the electronic technology field. Therefore, transparent conductors are so crucial that they control the intact mechanical flexibility, as the traditional metal oxides and glass substrates limit the flexibility. Thanks to CNT, high transparency, high conductivity, and better mechanical elasticity make them good candidates to replace conventional electrodes. When SWCNT was embedded with ploy imide PI (matrix with thickness 7 μm), a remarkable smooth surface and high transparent film was obtained [70]. Moreover, doping the SWCNT with MoOx improved the conductivity and the hole mobility. The obtained conductor offers a sheet resistance of 82 Ω sq.−1and 8 % transmission at 700 nm, resulting in a PCE of about 15.2 %. This foldable cell can withstand up to 10,000 folding cycles within a radius of 0.5 mm. Fig. 6
(a, b) showed the fabrication process of the ultrathin SWCNT/PI and the 3D illustration of the foldable fabricated PSCs. The device showed long term stability over 25 days upon storage in a nitrogen-filled glove box beneath the darkness condition at room temperature (≈25 °C).HTL-free perovskite MAPbI3 exhibits the self-recrystallization capability upon introducing oxygenated-functional groups into the CNTs [71]. These groups are bonded through acid treatment to enhance the chemical interaction at CNTs/perovskite interface. The perovskite film exhibited a remarkable enhancement on its morphology after long-term ageing. This report showed a relatively better efficiency after storage in 20–50 % humidity. After long-term aging, the initial PCE was 3.21–7.89 %, which was noticeably increased up to 9.54–12.14 %, due to an improved interface contact. These results confirmed the improved efficiency and stability performance, in addition to the lower charge-transporting resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanotubes in the perovskite film
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned before, the grain boundaries of the polycrystalline perovskite film impede the long diffusion way for charge transfer. So, utilizing SWCNTs can be used as bridging components that can be considered effective charge carrier-transporting channels. In addition, it acts as nucleation sites in the crystallization process and thus getting much larger grains that might provide better performance [72]. Herein, an experimental comparison study between different functionalized CNTs has been done, where pristine CNTs, amino-functionalized CNTs (NH3-CNTs) and carboxy-functionalized CNT (COOH-CNTs) were used as additives for the perovskite film [73]. The authors reported PCEs values as follows, 13.19 %, 16.56 %, and 17.47 % for pristine CNT, NH2-CNT, and COOH-CNT. The COOH-CNT-based device was selected for further investigations, improving the PCE of 18.68 % on controlling the concentration. On studying the device stability, COOH-CNT device maintained about 80 % of the initial PCE in the ambient atmosphere for 15 days at a relative humidity of 35 − 45 %. Moreover, Functionalization of CNTs with amino group could control the crystallinity and orientation of the perovskite crystal of MA0.85FA0.15PbI3. Adding methylammonium chloride also facilitated the crystal grain of the perovskite. CNT-NH2 acted as both a growth template and charge carrier channels that enhanced the crystal grain and the charge transfer. Therefore, this device achieved a power conversion energy of 21 % [72].

Multi-walled carbon nanotubes have been integrated into perovskite sensitizer layer to facilitate the charge transfer between perovskite grains [74]. Cheng group investigated the effect of MWCNT insertion on the perovskite crystallinity. They found that by employing sulfonate carbon nanotubes, the space between the grains was filled up and the grains became larger. The interaction between PbI2 and sulfonic acid resulted in the aggregation of methyl ammonium iodide molecules around s-CNT and then perovskite crystals formation. Further heat treatment of CNT leaded to a higher PCE of 15 % than the untreated CNT value of 10 %.

Generally, the 2D hybrid perovskites are recently better nominees to substitute the traditional 3D ones for PSCs owing to their well resistance to moisture and UV light. Nevertheless, the employing of 2D result in significantly lower efficiency due to the lack of density of charge carriers and hence, poor charge transport. High crystalline 2D perovskite film, almost a single crystal, should be synthesized to address this problem. The perovskite components are vertically aligned with the two other side contacts to facilitate charge transport [75]. Upon introducing SCNTs or graphene, the lifetime of the charge carriers increased, resulting in a 2-fold improvement in efficiency. Fig. 6 c shows the charge transfer dependence on the orientation; the obtained PCE and FF values were (9.03 % &6017 %) and (0.63&0.61) for PSC-2D SCNT and PSC-2D Gr, respectively, which were enhanced than that of PSC-2D values (4 % and 0.55). Sodium deoxycholate DOC was used as a surfactant to get better dispersion of single wall carbon nanotubes in water. However, a limited fill factor value was obtained. The researchers displaced DOC with another surfactant called 4,6-di(anthracen-9-yl)-1,3-phenylene bis (dimethyl −carbamate) and N, N-dimethylformamide as a dispersing medium. A PCE of 20 % was obtained that resulted from the improved perovskite grain size and thus enhanced passivation degree [76].

Graphene is depicted as “the thinnest, most flexible, and strongest material known”[27]. It is a 2D c-based material that is covalently bonded to assemble a honeycomb structure with a sp2 hybridization [78], making it strong and flexible. Numerous procedures have been adapted for the fabrication of graphene, including chemical, electrochemical, exfoliation, mechanical, and chemical vapor deposition (CVD) methods. Graphene is a zero-bandgap semi-metal where the π-π connection is the key behind its electrical properties. The delocalization of electrons results from the out-of-plan π-interaction of the Pz electrons that control the graphene conductivity. A monolayer of graphene can absorb UV-NIR light of about 2.3 % of the incident light, enabling it to be a good auxiliary layer in photovoltaic applications. Graphene exhibits carrier mobility up to 2 x 105 Cm2V-1s−1 Mahmoudi, Wang, et al. 2018), while at the Dirac points, the density of the state is too stumpy is favorable to control the carrier injection in the graphene [79]. Various molecular modifications are used to functionalize graphene to tune and improve its properties [80]. Graphene oxide is an example of a structure that results from the oxidation of graphene modified by some hydroxyl or epoxy functional groups at the hexagonal network sites and carboxylic groups at the edges [81]. The following section will dive into employing graphene and its derivatives in the different layers of perovskite solar cells, which are summarized in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene and its derivatives as HTLs
ce:section.ce:para:
Recent research has focused on the fabrication of more stable PSCs with alternative material designs. Both the moisture stability and charge mobility by utilizing of graphene derivatives are intensively investigated. Pristine graphene exhibit a work function of 4.5 eV, while reduced graphene (rGO) has 4.9 eV [82,83] plus the enhanced hole conductivity; their energy levels aligned with those of the perovskite VB (5.43 eV). When graphene derivatives were used as auxiliary buffer layers between the perovskite and the Spiro-OMeTAD, the results disclosed an improvement in the perovskite film protection, hole extraction, recombination resistance, wettability through Spiro-OMeTAD deposition, and better stability [84]. The contact angle vanished from 13.4 to 0.0-degree, Fig. 7
(a, b), and the GO saturated the unbounded Pb forming Pb-o, while the overall efficiency enhanced by 45 % to reach 15.1 %. In 2016, amino-functionalized graphene NGs had been utilized as an interlayer between the perovskite and the HTL by Wen; et al [85]. This NG-based interfacial modification resulted in a 36 % improved PCE with a maximum value of 14.6 %, which might be regarded that the amino groups reduced the surface trap of the perovskite and the charge recombination at the interface, while the built-in voltage increased indicating the improved driving force through the separation of charge carriers.

Interestingly, the green synthesis of graphitic carbon from the plant species called Eichhornia crassipes for the fabrication of HTL/CE in PSCs devices had a sustainable impact on device stability [86]. Introducing extracted EC-GC encapsulated perovskite layer helped quash the interface charge recombination and hamper the moisture destroying. Better air stability was achieved with maintaining its initial PCE up to 94.4 % even after 1000 h, which was thanks to the graphitization of the bio-prepared graphite that acts as a dual role HTL and an electrode. Esmaiel et al. have completely covered the upper and lower side of the perovskite film with graphene oxide-based ETL and HTL to protect it from the outer environmental conditions [87]. In their report, the graphene oxide has been employed into two composites; NiO/GO and Li-modified GO/TiO2 as HTL and ETL, respectively, for inverted p-i-n architecture PSCs. The PV parameters were measured to be 11.2 %, 0.62, 0.97 mV, and 18.6 mA/cm2 for PCE, FF, Voc, and Jsc, respectively. The PCE acquired during aging test was displayed in Fig. 7 c, by comparing the device under study FTO/NiO/GO/Perovskite/GO-Li/TiOx/Al with an organic-based device FTO/PEDOT: PSS/Perovskite/PCBM/Al through keeping them in dark-dry box under ambient air with humidity range 28–32 %. A fast deterioration was observed after a few days for the organic-based device losing around 90 % of its starting PCE, while the GO-based one retained up to 70 % of its initial performance. This might be ascribed to the introducing of GO/GO-Li layers that act as good shielding against the dispersion of moisture into the greatly sensitive active layer and thus maintain its performance under ambient conditions.

Reduced graphene oxide could be utilized due to the anti-recombination property, which increases the charge carrier lifetime. Palma et al. prepared rGO by a simple, efficient method to be utilized as HTL in mesoscopic PSCs [88]. From the stability tests conducted over 1987 h of shelf life achieving PCE of 6.62 % and light soaking for 120 h as shown in Fig. 7 C, they concluded that rGO-based PSC reached 26 % of the as-prepared PSC with higher stability compared to the Spiro-OMeTAD-based one (decrease one order of magnitude), revealing the optimal performance of rGO to reduce the charge recombination. JunJun Jin, et al. have fabricated compatible PSC with graphene-AgNWs/PET (polyethylene terephthalate)and hole-free, carbon-based back electrode [89]. The device with the configuration (PET)/(graphene–AgNWs)/SnO2/MAPbI3/Carbon, was successfully synthesized, achieving a PCE of 9.73 %. This flexible system demonstrated better mechanical robustness compared to their FTO counterparts. The photovoltaic parameters obtained were JSC = 16.48 mA/cm2, VOC = 0.956 V, and FF = 0.618, retaining 89 % of its initial performance after 1000 cycles which confirmed better reproducibility and stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene and its derivatives as ETLs
ce:section.ce:para:
As mentioned before, TiO2 was widely used as a typical electron transporting semiconducting material that extracted the photogenerated electrons from the perovskite active layer and then inject them into the FTO electrode. However, there are some carrier transporting limitations within at the perovskite /c-TiO2interface due to the presence of trap status density throughout the perovskite layer [24], consequently, leading to undesirable e/h recombination and durable current–voltage(J-V) hysteresis in planar architecture devices. To overcome these obstacles, some alternative low cost, more stable and reasonable efficient solutions have been intensively studied. Wang and co-workers introduced graphene into the titanium oxide nanoparticles, as alternative to the widely-employed m-TiO2, resulting in low temperature fabrication process at below 150⁰C [90]. The graphene nanoflakes presented outstanding charge-extraction and a PCE of 15 %. The highly conductive rGO was optimized in the rGO-m-TiO2 nanocomposite in which the optimal rGO volume was 0.4 vol% that was considered a simple solution to reduce the ETL/perovskite interfacial resistance [91] and improve the charge collection that was confirmed by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements. This composite showed an increase in the Jsc, FF, and Voc with a PCE of 14.5 %and diminished the large recombination compared to traditional m-TiO2based perovskite solar cells, Fig. 8
(a,b), showed the diagram of the fabricated PSC device and a cross-sectional SEM image of a rGO (0.4 vol%)/mp-TiO2. Moreover, the incorporation of Li atoms (4.3 eV) into the graphene oxide provides a worthy energy toning with the conduction band of TiO2 resulting in a remarkable improvement in the injection of electrons from the perovskite sensitizer into the m-TiO2-ETL [92].

Interestingly, graphene quantum dots exhibit distinguished size-dependent PL properties than other carbon derivatives plus photostability, low toxicity, bio computability, high quantum efficiency, and tunable fluorescence [93]. Zhu et al. investigated the role of graphene quantum dots GQDsasa superfast electron subway in the PSCs photovoltaic devices [94]. The insertion of ultrathin GQDs between the perovskite sensitizer and TiO2 layers facilitates the electron extraction time to get (90–160 ps) as shown in Fig. 8 (c, d). Loading the GQDs enhanced the photon-current conversion within the visible-NIR regions giving a PCE value of > 10 %. While on adding the graphene quantum dots GQDs as dopants into the mesoporous TiO2 with different ratios, Fig. 9
(a-d),the results showed a heightening to the PCE up to 50 % compared to the doped-free cells [95]. The 2.5 vol% GQD-based PSC gave Voc, Jsc and FF of values 0.97 V, 21.92 mA/cm2, and 0.63, respectively, in addition to maintaining 88 % of the initial PCE after 500 h under prolonged illumination at ambient conditions. EIS recorded the inhibition of recombination processes well as better charge transfer and electron injection between the ETL and perovskite layers, which is indeed regarded to the exceptional properties of the GQDs including the quantum size effect and high conductivity.

The lithium-neutralized graphene (Li-GO) exhibits an enhancement to the PV parameters; for Jsc (+10.5 %), the fill factor by (+7.5 %) and a reduction in the device J-V curves hysteresis to about 50 %. Notable stability to the non-encapsulated device under study against prolonged illumination stemming mainly from passivation of the oxygen vacancies regarding the TiO2 that is responsible for the moisture attack. Moreover, on utilization of (Li-GO) with the graphene-doped TiO2(G-m-TiO2) as ETL into MAPbI3-based PSC, Biccari et al. found an increase regarding the efficiency of the electron collection up to threefold higher than standard m-TiO2, based on the picosecond PL techniques [96]. Different types of devices were fabricated using various combinations of ETLs as follow: mTiO2, G + mTiO2, mTiO2 plus GO-Li interlayer and G + mTiO2 plus GO-Li interlayer, shown in Fig. 9 e. They also observed a much better MAPbI3 crystalline quality with frozen tetragonal phase, whatever the temperature was, and a lower trap density than the other investigated samples.

Obviously, these graphene-based ETLs are considered favorable candidates for the development of more stable PSCs devices. For the planar architecture PSCs, upon insertion the three dimensional rG Scaffold between TiO2 and the perovskite absorber layer, the PV performance increased by 27 % as compared to the traditional device [97]. The obtained rG scaffold-based device showed enhanced efficiency, suppressed hysteresis and the PCE was about 17.2 %. This obtained efficiency dropped by only 20 % after a month which showed enhanced stability than TiO2-based one with 65 % loss. ZnO was rarely employed as ETL as the surface hydroxide groups induced the decomposition to the perovskite film into PbI2 during the annealing process. However, reduced graphene oxide QDs can be utilized with ZnO as a quasi-core shell structure to tackle this problem [97]. Here, rGO not only protected the ZnO surface but also it boosted the rapid extraction of the generated species from the perovskite film to suppress recombination.

In addition, SnO2 was employed as an outstanding ETL for obtaining highly efficient organic–inorganic hybrid PSCs as it possesses a lower conduction band (CB) and higher carrier mobility. However, the low-temperature process resulted in an increase in the trap-state density and J-V hysteresis. Modification of SnO2-based ETL by a highly conductive, 2D material like naphthalene diimide-graphene would enhance the surface hydrophobicity in addition to the creation of Vander Waals interaction at the lead halide perovskite-ETL interface [98]. This behavior resulted in a fixed octahedral [PbI6]4- and highly efficient PCE of about 20 % with an enhanced fill factor of 82 %. Zhu et al. have investigated another attempt by using graphene ink doped SnO2, as a result, the device exhibited a remarkable efficiency of about 18 %, and superior stability with retaining∼90 % efficiency during aging in air and humid atmosphere for 300 h, which might be attributed to the hydrophobicity of graphene [99]. Kakavelakis et al. have successfully doped [6,6]-Phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester PCBM-based ETL with rGO into planar inverted perovskite (MAPbI3−xClx and MAPbI3), achieving an progress of 12 % with a non-hysteresis PCE of 14.5 % [100]. The spectroscopic characterization demonstrated that the addition of rGO resulted in the increased ETL conductivity, and thus higher Jsc and FF values and a decrease in series resistance plus the surface roughness. The rGO leaded to suppressing the surface trap and passivation of the perovskite surface and thus higher Voc was obtained. Moreover, the presence of rGO facilitated the extraction of the charge carriers from the perovskite to the metal electrode and therefore reducing the light soaking effect. These results, Fig. 9 (f,g), demonstrated the stability of the rGO-based perovskite even intensive aging. Moreover, to enhance fullerene-based ETLs a scalable strategy was successfully processed by incorporating high-quality graphene flakes (GFs) into PCBM [101]. The turnout of GFs in the new composite (GF: PCBM) suppressed recombination leading to an improvement PCE of 20.8 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene and its derivatives as counter electrodes (CEs)
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is one of the most promising candidates for foldable PV device fabrication, because of its high conductivity, smooth surface, and high transparency that enable light absorption from both sides. In 2015, Peng You, et al. ensured the trend of flexible PSCs by fabricating graphene-based semi-transparent as the top electrode for PSCs via chemical vapor deposition method CVD [102]. They dramatically enhanced the conductivity of graphene to (4.5 eV) by coating it with a thin layer of poly-(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene): poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT: PSS) (5 eV). This strategy can generate holes in the graphene film by the phenomena electrostatic doping, achieving optimal PCE of 12 % and 11 % during illuminating the device from the FTO (bottom side) and graphene (top side), respectively. The doping materials also act as an adhesive layer through the lamination process of graphene at the top of the perovskite by forming van der Waal bonding with the spiro-based layer. The factor of the number of graphene layers has been also discussed; the system with two layers of graphene showed the highest PCE and more stable devices. Graphene-based electrodes are expected to be applied in flexible PSCs by either printing or role-to-role methods. Super flexible PSCs were investigated using graphene as a transparent conducting anode, instead of the typical ITO anode, reaching an efficiency of 16.8 % with no hysteresis. The overwhelming flexible devices revealed outstanding stability against bending deformation, conserving > 90 % and 85 % of their original efficiency under severe mechanical testing of 1000 and 5000 bending cycles, respectively, with a bending radius of 2 mm without significant cracks [103]. Even though graphene exhibits lower conductivity compared to that of ITO, it demonstrates favored-energy-level alignment with the HOMO level of the most common HTL PEDOT:PSS in addition to the enhanced optical transmittance and superior mechanical strength indicating that the graphene-based electrode is a promising alternative to traditional ITO electrodes [104].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene and its derivatives for the perovskite layer
ce:section.ce:para:
The perovskite film, usually inserted between the ETL and HTL layers, acts as the light absorber and transportation medium for ambipolar charges; its quality performance depends mainly on some key factors like morphology, thickness and grain size, which greatly affect the net PCE. Numerous reports demonstrated that large grain size and thick perovskite films are more favorable for higher PCE performance. Ahra Yi et al. have first introduced a more stable and flexible graphene-encapsulated- perovskite through the roll-based dry transfer routs achieving superior long-dated stability by retaining 82 % of its incipient efficacy after 3700 h in ambient air [105]. When the device was exposed to 85–90 % humidity, it retained 95 % of its initial performance after 100 h-illumination. They also studied the stability performance as a function of graphene layer numbers. From the results of XRD and UV- absorption, 2–3 graphene layers achieved higher efficiency to passivate the water and oxygen molecules from penetrating into the device. Fig. 10
(a-c) shows the obtained results of this study. Gidey and Kim added, for the first time, functionalized graphene oxide to the perovskite precursor PbI2 for tuning the perovskite layer crystallization rate [106]. The optimized concentration of chlorine-functionalized graphene oxide (Cl-GO) was 5.0 vol% with a solar-electrical PCE of 15 %, for the inverted p-i-n-device configuration was FTO/NiOx/MAPbI3 + Cl–GO/PCBM/BPC/Ag. The results of comparing the optimum condition (Cl-GO 5.0 vol%) to the reference condition without Cl-GO are summarized below. (1) Absorbance: 2.58 vs. 1.99 at 450 nm and 1.33 vs. 1.07 at 550 nm, representing a 24.0 % increase. (2) Film thickness increased by 24 % (383 nm vs. 308 nm). (3) Crystallite size (t) was 130 nm vs. 118 nm for the (110) plane and 75 nm vs. 65 nm for the (220) plane, representing a 10–15 % increase. (4) The average domain size increased by approximately 223 % from 160 nm to 517 nm. (5) The average PCE is 14.72 %, up from 12.26 %, representing a 20 % rise. Therefore, we conclude that Cl-GO is an excellent additive. Nitrogen-doped graphene oxide also has a compatible effect on suppressing the perovskite crystallization and inducing the grain size [107,108].

Computational studies for the effect of graphene sheets on the stability of the MASnI2, based on the first principle calculations of the density function theory, have been studied by Zhihua Hu et al. [109]. The results showed a van der Waals interaction created at the G/MASnI2 heterojunction interface, resulting in an electron transfer pathway and a preferable built-in electric field for effective charge separation. The adsorption energy calculation established the low affinity of the graphene for water molecules than the perovskite film, demonstrating the creation of energy barriers for water diffusion of 9 eV value. Therefore, graphene could act as a passive-block layer inhibiting the penetration of moisture into the perovskite structure and thus enhance the perovskite stability.

Eri Widianto et al., investigated the optical properties of GO in conjunction with MAPbI3.Intensive reports have been carried out to deal with the challenge of how to passivate perovskite crystals from degradation by utilizing graphene oxide and rGO [110,111]. Silver nanoparticles incorporated with rGO is another example that addresses perovskite film's instability problem [112]. Under ambient conditions of the room temperature and 45–50 % humidity, the Ag-rGO retained up to 100 % of initial parameters values after aging over 330 days. The Ag nanoparticles exploit to increase the work function of rGO (4.22 to 4.95 eV) in addition to inducing the carrier mobility and conductivity. Moreover, it enhanced the thermal stability; when the system was aged for 90 h at 90C, it retained 94 % of its initial PCE. Fig. 10 (e-i) depicts the Schematic of the perovskite/Ag-rGO composite-based PSCs and the graphic of blocking of iodide ions and moisture using a graphene sheet, making it a promising candidate for the long-stability PSCs.

Fullerene is another C-allotrope in which the carbon atoms are connected via single and double bonds with fused rings of 5–7 atoms. It is widely investigated in optoelectronics in general and PSCs in specific. It produces no hysteresis and does not need a high annealing temperature. Today, PEDOT: PSS or NiO-based materials are the most HTLs commonly used, while ETL materials are widely based on fullerene derivatives. Due to the room temperature processing of fullerene derivatives and their dissolvability in nonpolar organic solvents, they are successfully used as an alternative solution for the high annealed m-TiO2 (>450C) to conserve the absorber perovskite layer from degradation. Typical examples of fullerene are PC60BM ([6,6]-Phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester) and PC70BM ([6,6]-Phenyl-C71-butyric acid methyl ester) that can be functionalized to over tune their properties. Wang et al. reported how a double layer of evaporated fullerene and spun PCBM could mitigate the dark current leakage, in addition to how the trap density of state could be eliminated [49,113]. Most fullerene derivatives exhibit remarkable electron mobility enabling them to work as effective promising ETLs to achieve high-performance stable PSCs. Guo et al. first studied the feasibility of PC61BM-based ETL in conventional PHJ architecture achieving 3.9 % PCE performance [114]. The PCE significantly reached 12–13 % by optimizing the perovskite deposition through the annealing conditions and the solvent additives by Jen and co-workers [115]. By well-controlling the thickness of the perovskite film, Jangwon Seo, et al. have optimized the performance over 14 % [116]. To tackle the problem of the perovskite roughness-surface morphology, thicker layer of PCBM can be used to prevent deterioration of perovskite owing to the direct contact with the back electrode; the optimized thickness reported in this article was 55 nm. Different fullerene derivatives were studied as ETLs like; C60, PC61BM, and indene-C60busductIC60BA by Po-Wei Lian, et al. [117] . as atrial to recognize the interaction between the perovskite and the fullerene. The three fullerene-based PSC devices were depicted in Fig. 11
a; the obtained PCEs were 8.06 %, 13.37, and 15.445 for C60, IC60BA and PC61BM, respectively. The obtained electron motility values of the fullerene derivatives elucidated the bulk transport that boosted charge dissociation through the device. However, C60 possesses lower LUMO than PC61BM, both exhibited comparable Voc are (89, 92 V, respectively), indicating that the presence of tinny misfit in the energy orientation at perovskite/fullerene interface could be compensated by the high electron mobility. However, PCBM has some drawbacks, including poor electron mobility, current leakage, and nonradiative recombination at the interface of perovskite/PCBM due to tiny relative permittivity resulting in electron capture regions [118]. Therefore, doping PCBM with some effective additives is another alternative solution to overcome these limitations, or multiple layers with the fullerene can be employed to form a double/triple layer cathode buffer. By replacing methyl hydrogen in the PCBM structure with a phenyl group to obtain [6,6]-phenyl-C61-butyric acid benzyl ester (PCBB), the resulting efficiency approached 20 % [119]. This fabricated ETL exhibited a noticeable self-aggregation that promoted stability for 600 h under continuous irradiation, retaining about 90 % of its initial performance. Fig. 11 (b, c) shows the effect of intermolecular interaction on the stability performance of the device. The electron transfer between the C60 was facilitated by inducing the non-covalent intermolecular interaction, consequently, the electron extraction and reducing recombination were achieved. Meanwhile, on employing the organic semiconductors like perylene diimide diazide PDI-DA, together with the fullerene (C60)-based PCBM, as ETL, the resulting efficiency reached 12 % and electron mobility (>10−4 cm2 V−1 s−1). This cross-linked film was implemented in both the n-i-p structure and the inverted p-i-n PSC in the presence of a CsFAPbIBr active layer for obtaining more robustness [120]. The p-i-n structure showed promoted performance than n-i-p structure. On talking about the ambient stability of the produced cross-linked ETL-based PSC, the device remained around 60 % of its initial performance over 50 days under 80 % humidity without any further encapsulation, as shown in Fig. 12
a. Additionally, Lingbo Jia, et al. have applied a novel auxiliary interlayer bis-dimethylamino-functionalized fullerene derivative (PCBDMAM) and formed PCBM/PCBDMAM double fullerene-cathode buffer layers (CBLs), Fig. 12 (b-e) [121]. Insertion of PCBDMAM created a dipole layer between PCBM and Ag electrode, which enhanced the durability of the MAPbI3-based iPSC, and remarkable increase in the PCE from 14 % (PVBM only) into 18 %. PCBDMAM offered higher hydrophobicity than pure PCBM causing better ambient stability. This fabricated device could sustain about 67 % of its original performance after 1440 h storage without any encapsulation at ambient atmosphere.

Jing Yan Liu et al. have employed fullerene C60 as ETL together with SnO2(as a spacer layer between C60 and Ag electrode), resulting in a champion long-term stability over 90 days (> 2100 h) maintaining 80 % of its original PCE (16 %) in atmospheric aging and heating at 85C [122]. The significant high performance may be attributed to the passivation effect of C60 and the matched energy level of the high crystalline SnO2. When Fullerene end-capped polyethylene glycol derivative (PCBPEG) was used as additive to the unencapsulated perovskite, the device PCE reached 17 % with enhanced stability [123]. The perovskite film exhibited large grain size, suppressed the e-trap state density, and enhanced electric properties, which improved the overall PV performance. Upon aging the device at room temperature for 360 h in 32 % humidity, it maintained more than 70 % of its initial performance. The electron communication at the perovskite/ETL interface is greatly affected by the electron mobility and the conductivity of the ETL material. The earlier this year, Tian Zheng and co-workers designed fullerene-4-(1-(4-(tert-butyl)phenyl)pyrrolidin-2-yl)benzene-minimum (C60-tBu-I) as an auxiliary layer functioned to accelerate the electron transporting and hinder charge recombination [124]. This fullerene-based system achieved a PCE over 17 % and a FF value of 77 %. The long-term stability was significantly improved by introducing the bulk t-butyl that increased the hydrophobicity of the perovskite and thus the water resistance, the contact angle was shown in Fig. 12 (f,g), and maintained about 87 % of its primary efficiency during aging for 500 h in ambient air of moisture 40–60 %.

With employing all-metal oxides-based transporting layers, the imperfect surface limitations can be adopted by anchoring the metal oxide with fullerene to get more efficient and stable PSC device. Kai Yao, et al. have anchored ZnO nanocrystals by fullerene nano-shells as an efficient ETL and utilized NiOx as a HTL achieving 21 % with remarkable enhanced performance longevity [126]. The presence of highly strong binding of fullerene C60-substituted catechol (Fa) enabled the metal oxide to permeate the perovskite material before crystallization leading to a sensitized system with n-type configuration in addition to passivation of the surface hydroxyl group by Zn-O-C bonding. Upon 1 sun exposure without UV- filtering, the device retained 82 % after two aging steps for 500 h at room temperature (RT) and for an additional 500 h at 65C in ambient conditions with 40 % humidity. Fig. 13
(a-c) shows the device configuration of fullerene-anchored core–shell ZnO nanoparticles, J-V curve, and the device stability over 500 h. On the other hand, fluoroalkyl-substituted fullerene/perovskite heterojunction (f-FPHJ) showed outstanding stability on storage for 1 month, maintaining about 83 % of its original performance (18 %) in 60 % humidity without capsulation, Fig. 13 (d, e) [127], when used as a perovskite-passivation layer together with PCBM. The presence of fluoroalkyl group effectively reduced the surface energy of C60 and improved the hydrophobicity resulting in a contact angle of 113°. The presence of fullerene derivative functionalized with a carboxylic group (CPTA) could modify the fasten between SnO2 and MAPbI3, delivering a long-dated stability for a month and 16 days upon storage in ambient air with 30 % humidity percent [128], retaining 87 % of its initial photovoltaic performance. Moreover, the hydrophilic C60 pyrrolidine trisacid (CPTA)-based ETL demonstrated better durability on mechanical bending up to 500 cycles with a curvature radius of 5 mm. This flexible planar architecture with 32 nm CPTA-modified SnO2, 100 nm Spiro-OMeTAD, and 330 nm MAPbI3 caused a power conversion efficiency of over 18 %. Fig. 14
(a-c) shows the Molecular structure of CPTA, Band alignment between various layers and normalized rendering parameters of the fabricated flexible MAPbI3 solar cell upon growing the curvature cycles. The carboxylic group at the fullerene chain can form chemical bonds across the uncoordinated Sn on the surface of SnO2, which was investigated by the XPS analysis. A promising n-doped fullerene derivatives-based ETL has been fabricated for planar heterojunction n-i-p PSC devices that can be prepared at low-temperature routs (<100°C) [129]. This n-doped material composed of (4-(1,3-dimethyl-2,3-dihydro-1H-benzoimidazol-2-yl) phenyl)dimethylamine with fullerene derivatives resulted in high stable PTEG-1 matrix, hysteresis free-PCE, better conductivity, lower work function. Moreover, the device could sustain about 85 % of the incipient PCE (18.5 %) after 1000 h storage in 20 % humidity at 60C.

Another effective strategy has been taken to overcome the poor electron mobility of the fullerene by mixing n-type polymeric material together with PCBM to form homogeneous bulk-mixed (HBM)attaining a comprehensive coverage on the perovskite film with suitable energy levels [118]. The electron capture radius of the resulting film reduced to 12.5 nm from 14.8 nm of PCBM owing to the higher relative permittivity thus mitigating the recombination at the perovskite/ETL interface. The efficiency of this fabricated device exceeded 20 % and FF of 82 %. Furthermore, the stability was boosted for 45 days in ambient air conditions maintaining 80 % of the original efficiency. Fig. 14 (d-g) showed the structure of the fabricated inverted HBM-based PSC and the stability results of the obtained device.

Functionalization of fullerene derivatives with gold nanoparticles to form intermediate layer C60-BCT@Au, Fig. 14 (h-k), can deliver better perovskite crystallinity in addition to reducing charge carrier recombination at the fasten modification between TiO2-ETL and perovskite [130]. The gold atom bonded through a fully conjugated linker, greatly enhancing the electron extraction and transfer. This unencapsulated cell could retain about 83 % of the original PCE (19 % with negligible hysteresis) after exposure to UV illumination for 500 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon black and carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, introducing carbon quantum dots CQDs into the perovskite has recently gained great attention for improving PV performance. CQDs could act as a passivation layer to the uncoordinated lead at the boundaries, reducing the recombination of non-radiative charge [131]. Higher PL intensities and longer lifetimes of the charge carriers could be obtained by introducing CQDs additives. The various functional groups of CQDs play a critical factor in perovskite film formation. During the perovskite crystallization process, the functional groups of CQDs can suppress the charged defects in the perovskite. For the first time, p-type CQDs were utilized in the based light emitting diodes that gave promising results for perovskite [132].

Two carbon sources, acetone A and the anhydrous acetic acid AC, were used to prepare A-CQD and AC-CQD by Tingting Xu [133]. These CQDs were prepared with/without nitrogen functionalities and then were utilized as additives in hole-free, carbon-based PSCs. The A-CQD showed good performance of (13.28 %) than that AC-CQD (7.85 %), while the pristine-reference PSC displayed only (10 %). They have demonstrated that A-CQDs interacted with MAPbI3 boost the crystal grain growth rate and thus obtaining more large size. The charge carrier transfer was facilitated, as well. On testing the long-term stability at 40–45 % humidity, room temperature and for 200 h, the PCE values were 36.6 %, 80.2 % and 90 % from the initial values for the pristine, CA-CQD, and A-CQD, respectively. Fig. 15
(a-e) shows the prepared carbon quantum dots (CQDs) preparation schemes. Light absorption by the sensitizer perovskite can be tuned to reduce the parasitic absorption losses resulting from the presence of metal oxide, a glass substrate, and the other functional layers. CQDs, with down converting characteristics, can convert high energetic UV- light to lower energy visible photons, thus enhancing light harvesting efficiency. Askar A. Maxim has coated the illuminated-transparent side of the glass substrate by CQDs-based layer using the deionized water as a new dispersion medium for the CQDs [134].

Integration of CQDs into inverted PSC also enhances the hole extraction at the perovskite/HTL contact. Daniele Benetti and co-workers, utilized CQDs to modify GO-HTL [135]. The resulting efficiency improved from 14.6 % to 16.2 % with the optimized CQDs content. Furthermore, upon employing CCQDs as a downshifting layer with UV-range absorption, the PCE was further improved to 16.8 %. The hole extraction and transfer were enhanced by applying CQDs and hence this delayed recombination that was confirmed by the transient photovoltage (TPV) and transient PL decay. The normalized PCE maintained about 62 % of the initial value after continuous operation in the full radiation for 30 min which is more than that of sole GO (48 %) with the same conditions of 40–50 % humidity without encapsulation. Fig. 15 h shows the charge transfer and charge recombination mechanism for GO and GO/C dots to study the hole-extraction kinetics at the HTL/perovskite interface.

The carbon dots CDs have been investigated to give a promising PSCs performance. When they were synthesized from an optimized ratio of the precursors citric acid and the ethylenediamine and then and then deposited on the ITO films [136], remarkable retardant in the ITO work function from 4.64 eV to 3.42 eV was shown by those amine functionalized CDs. While carboxyl-based CDs increased the WF to 4.9 eV. The resultant PCE increased from 17.3 % to 19.5 % giving a promising candidate for the PSCs performance enhancement. Red-carbon quantum dots CQDs were incorporated into SnO2, resulting in outstanding electron mobility enhancement by 20-fold [137]. Superior increase in PCE to reach 22.77 % owing to the passivation behavior to the traps at the perovskite/ETL interface and helping in formation highly pure, uniform crystalline perovskite. Upon employing these excellent CQDs in highly efficient ETL, the long-term stability was also improved that maintained over 95 % of the premier value after 1000 h storage in 40–60 % moisture at 25C. Spherical CQDs-doped PCBM was utilized as highly efficient-stable ETL for the inverted perovskite, resulted in optimized PCE of 18.1 % [138]. The characterization tools like electronic impedance spectroscopy EIS, conductive atomic force microscopy AMF, and ultrafast spectroscopy elucidated that CQDs-PCBM could enhance the charge extraction, electron mobility, and electrical conductivity. Moreover, these CQDs could help passive iodide ions I- from undergoing diffusion at the interface, improving the device stability. Fig. 15 (f, g) displayed the proposed mechanism of preventing the perovskite film by CQDs and the long-term stability curve. For testing the device's durability after 20 days later, the device maintained about 70 % in dry air. Grazing-incidence XRD (GIXRD) confirmed the absence of the AgI peaks within the pattern of CQDs-based device. More and more research have been conducted to attain highly efficient PSCs with improved environmental stability. Qiang Guo and co-workers prepared CQDs as an additive that block water molecules and the perovskite from contact [139], even if aging the device in ambient atmosphere without humidity control for about 4 months, while the MAPbI3 sustain its black color. The PCE with the optimum CQDs concentration reached 19.38 % PCE, indicating a good passivation to the perovskite layer and thus improving the overall PV parameters [140].

Finally, this thorough discussion highlights the recent development of C-based materials and to what extent CNT, graphene, and fullerene positively impact the PSCs' performance. By being integrated into each layer of the solar cells, the carrier transportation layers (ETL and HTL), the electrodes, or interface modifiers in different architectures, these carbon materials enhance the device properties and stability towards different factors, leading to a clear improvement in efficiency. While most research focuses on increasing the PSCs' efficiency, stability should now take precedence. The current performance levels of PSCs need further improvements for commercialization. There are several key concerns to be addressed. Therefore, CNT, graphene, and fullerene present a high chance of being an effective part of future PSC research. Furthermore, future research should focus on alternatives that boost potential cost-effective mass production. Carbon materials are employed as outer electrodes instead of high-cost materials like gold and silver, which consequently reduces cell costs. However, the misalignment between the carbon-based electrodes and the perovskite layer may result in a decline in the cell performance. Thus, regulating the energy levels and the interface engineering of carbon and the perovskite phase can be the solution for improving device parameters [141]. Modeling and simulation to deeply understand the charge transfer mechanism and the interaction between the perovskite and the c-based materials could result in design optimization.

Although CNT exhibits significant mechanical, optical, and electrical properties, single-walled CNTs still suffer from a lack of conductivity and transmittance. More research is needed, such as doping with other materials such as conductive polymers, etc., to be included in the charge transfer layers and remarkably reduce charge recombination and thus boost their performance. Moreover, extensive research should be carried out in functionalizing CNTs via different groups to enhance the perovskite layer's growth. Additionally, improving the optical transmittance enables promising CNTs in the bifacial PSCs to be employed as both back and front electrodes instead of metal electrodes. We must better understand how the CNTs characteristics impact the device's performance and durability.

Graphene and its derivatives, such as graphene oxide GO, reduced graphene oxide rGO, graphene quantum dots, and functionalized graphene, are other significant C-based materials that played a pivotal role in improving PSCs in terms of stability and efficiency. They possess remarkable charge mobility and extraction, low recombination levels and significant conductivity, and optical, mechanical, and hydrophobic properties. Conversely, several key challenges need intensive delve to realize graphene benefits and maximize device performance entirely. Fabrication of cost-effective, high-quality graphene is critical for large-scale applications. Current methods, such as chemical vapor deposition or epitaxial growth, are still unconventional to mass production. However, imperfections like grain boundaries and vacancies may cause deterioration in its electrical and optical properties. Moreover, introducing a controlled thin film of graphene derivatives into the different layers of the device with feasible and economic methods is another issue. Therefore, tailoring graphene via functionalization, doping (e.g., Nitrogen), or incorporation with other metal oxide charges may tune the charge transfer capability and the work function, resulting in better compatibility between them and the adjacent device layers.

PSCs are of considerable interest as promising renewable energy conversion sources, owing to the simplicity of fabrication, high PCE, and low cost compared to the other types of photovoltaic. However, device stability and mass production still hamper the commercialization of PSCs, and thus these issues should be addressed. PSC designs have found several uses for one-dimensional carbon nanotubes (CNTs) due to their exceptional electrical, optical, and mechanical properties. Carbon nanoforms like carbon nanotubes, graphene derivatives, fullerene, and carbon quantum dots play an essential role in enhancing the PSCs performance, including charge carriers (e/or h) extraction and transfer, control of perovskite crystallinity and morphology, preventing humidity penetration, inhibiting current hysteresis, and replacing the traditional metal electrodes for flexible devices. One of the interesting approaches to enhancing electron collection efficiency is the insertion of CNT into the structure of the semiconductor. So, different papers doped ETL (such as TiO2, ZnO, and SnO2) with CNTs. Enhanced optical absorbance in perovskite films fabricated onto MWCNT-modified ETL films had been observed with enlarged grains and a red shift in the optical band gap. Furthermore, the PSCs have higher operational and thermal stability and higher efficiency. On the other hand, CNT provides good morphology and crystallinity for the HTL. It also has replaced Li-TFSI and other dopants due to their chemical stability.

Further research is needed to fully understand and characterize the charge transfer mechanism at the CNT/perovskite contact. Understanding the impact of CNT electrical types on charge extraction and transport is crucial for developing high-performance PSCs. CNTs are effective in increasing the longevity of PSCs. However, PSCs based on CNT CEs still have lower photovoltaic performance than Au-based devices. Improving interface engineering and lowering sheet resistances are necessary for efficient charge extraction, collection, and transport. To improve charge separation and photovoltaic performance of PSCs or carrier transport layer-free PSCs based on CNT CEs, consider constructing perovskite phase junctions, such as homojunction and heterojunction. CNT-based inorganic PSCs have potential for use in severe settings like near-space and deserts.

Functionalized CNTs can be used as a template to create larger, firmly orientated perovskite grains, leading to longer charge lifetimes and lower defect densities in perovskite films. As a result, the development of some polymers with molecular structures or special functional groups is urgently needed to wrap or graft on the surface of SWCNTs, to improve their solubility while also achieving additional functions, such as optimizing perovskite layer properties and reducing lead exposure risk. This effort would be a promising step towards developing efficient, stable, and environmentally sustainable PSCs. CNTs have been successfully employed as interlayers in perovskite/HTL, HTL/metal electrode, and perovskite/carbon electrode interfaces. Their one-dimensional structure can function as charge channels, facilitating interfacial hole transfer. It is worth noting that hybrid or functionalized CNTs can improve the dynamics and stability of PSCs, but these materials must be carefully selected and constructed to enable real improvements in PSC performance. Moreover, the potential of the CNT-based interlayer in perovskite/ETL should also be explored to reduce defect densities of both perovskite and common ETLs. Anyway, considerable follow-up efforts should be made in several aspects, including CNTs and perovskite materials, device structures, and interface engineering, to further promote photovoltaic performance and long-term stability of CNT-based PSCs, making them competitive in photovoltaic fields.

Moreover, graphene derivatives presented an awe-inspiring performance due to their remarkable charge mobility and tunable energy bias between the perovskite and charge-selective layers. Intensive research has also focused on another important carbon allotrope, fullerene, which has been discussed. Fullerene derivatives have been widely investigated as competitive additives for ETL either in conventional or inverted planar configurations. The insertion of fullerene into the PSCs worked on decreasing charge recombination and improving open-circuit voltage (VOC). From all discussed reports, it can be concluded that fullerenes (C60) are currently the most effective carbon compound for electron transport in perovskite solar cells. Graphene is a notable choice for flexibility and mechanical properties because of its excellent flexibility and stability, particularly in flexible solar cells. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are fantastic for boosting electron and hole transport, but they must be carefully integrated. As a result, the “best” material is determined by the carbon material's specific purpose (e.g., electron transport layer, electrode, or hole transport material) and the design of the perovskite solar cell (e.g., flexible or stiff, single-junction or tandem).

Alaa A. Sery: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Alaa E. Abd El-Samad: Methodology, Conceptualization. Radwa S. Mostafa: Writing – review & editing. Hager H. Zeenelabden: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Ahmed Mourtada Elseman: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Sajid Sajid: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Formal analysis. Mohamed M. Rashad: Visualization, Validation. Mostafa El-Aasser: Visualization, Validation.
